title: Logical Operators 🔗
description: Combine multiple conditions using AND, OR, and NOT.

slides.title: Combining Questions 🧩
slides.content:
# Complex Decisions

Sometimes, asking just one question isn't enough. 

Imagine a website's login screen. To log someone in, you must check TWO things:
1. Is the username correct?
2. Is the password correct?

You only want to let them in if **both** answers are `true`. 

JavaScript gives us **Logical Operators** to combine multiple Booleans together!

slides.title: The AND Operator (&&) 🤝
slides.content:
## Both Must Be True

The logical AND operator is written as two ampersands: **`&&`**.

It checks multiple conditions. It returns `true` ONLY if **every single condition** is true. If even one is false, the whole thing becomes false.

```javascript
true && true   // true
true && false  // false
false && false // false
```

Example: `(age >= 18) && (hasTicket === true)`

slides.title: Logical AND Test 💻
slides.content: Try changing one of the conditions to false and see what happens:

quizData.question: What is the result of testing: true && false && true?
quizData.explanation: The AND (&&) operator requires ALL conditions to be true. Since one is false, the whole expression becomes false.

slides.title: The OR Operator (||) 🔀
slides.content:
## Just One Is Enough

The logical OR operator is written as two vertical pipes: **`||`** (usually found right above the Enter key).

It returns `true` if **AT LEAST ONE** condition is true. It only returns `false` if every single condition is false.

```javascript
true || false  // true
false || true  // true
false || false // false
```

Example: `(today === "Saturday") || (today === "Sunday")`

quizData.question: You get a discount if you are a student OR a senior. Which operator do you use?
quizData.explanation: Because you only need to meet ONE of the conditions to get the discount, you use the OR (||) operator.

slides.title: The NOT Operator (!) 😈
slides.content:
## Reversing Logic

The logical NOT operator is written as a single exclamation mark: **`!`**.

It is the simplest operator: it just **flips** a Boolean to its opposite.

```javascript
!true  // false
!false // true
```

It's very useful for checking if something is NOT the case.

Example: `!isGameOver` means "If the game is NOT over".

quizData.question: What is the output of: console.log(!false);
quizData.explanation: The NOT operator (!) simply flips the Boolean. The opposite of false is true.
